intro: The pivotal question between Continue and Aider is how much of your setup stays portable: Continue carries low lock-in and Aider carries high lock-in. Continue is open-source IDE AI assistant for VS Code and JetBrains with swappable models. Aider is terminal AI pair programmer that edits files via Git commits. Pick Continue when the job is apps and you accept low lock-in; pick Aider when it is apps and you accept high lock-in. The data we have shows Continue at mostly transparent pricing and docs with a polished developer experience, and Aider at mostly transparent pricing and docs with solid developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Continue is a poor fit for simple projects, and Aider is a poor fit for simple projects. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list. If your team already lives inside the ecosystem one of them assumes, that default usually wins — migration is cheap to start and expensive later.
quick_take: Continue is for open-source IDE AI assistant; Aider is for terminal AI pair programmer that edits files; decide based on how portable the setup must stay.
verdict_a: Choose Continue if per-seat pricing at $20 / seat / month fits your team size and the work maps to open-source IDE AI assistant for VS Code and JetBrains with swappable models.
verdict_b: Choose Aider if deep integration is an acceptable price for the depth it buys and the work lines up with terminal AI pair programmer that edits files via Git commits.

faq.a: We can't verify it cleanly: Continue starts at $20 / seat / month (seat), while Aider (freemium) doesn't publish an entry price. Request a quote from Aider before assuming either side is cheaper.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Continue to Aider?
faq.a: Moving from Continue to Aider is feasible, but Aider is rated high lock-in — expect your integrations and workflows to need rework to match its model. Budget for a parallel-run period rather than a hard cutover.

Continue scores higher on developer experience in our data (5/5 vs 4/5). That reflects public feedback on docs, onboarding, and day-to-day ergonomics. Still, run a spike with your own code — DX ratings don't capture every edge.

faq.q: Is Aider a good alternative to Continue?
faq.a: Yes — Aider is a reasonable alternative to Continue for apps. The practical differences are seat-vs-freemium billing and low-vs-high lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Aider as a credible swap.
